CSGuy57: Here is a YAMS example of an arm. Consider this code likely to be the best way to implement things like setting the angle: 
// Copyright (c) FIRST and other WPILib contributors.
// Open Source Software; you can modify and/or share it under the terms of
// the WPILib BSD license file in the root directory of this project.

package frc.robot.subsystems;

import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Amps;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Degrees;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.DegreesPerSecond;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.DegreesPerSecondPerSecond;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Feet;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Second;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Seconds;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Volts;

import com.ctre.phoenix6.hardware.TalonFX;
import edu.wpi.first.math.controller.ArmFeedforward;
import edu.wpi.first.math.system.plant.DCMotor;
import edu.wpi.first.units.measure.Angle;
import edu.wpi.first.units.measure.AngularVelocity;
import edu.wpi.first.units.measure.Current;
import edu.wpi.first.units.measure.Voltage;
import edu.wpi.first.wpilibj2.command.Command;
import edu.wpi.first.wpilibj2.command.SubsystemBase;
import org.littletonrobotics.junction.AutoLog;
import org.littletonrobotics.junction.AutoLogOutput;
import org.littletonrobotics.junction.Logger;
import yams.gearing.GearBox;
import yams.gearing.MechanismGearing;
import yams.mechanisms.config.ArmConfig;
import yams.mechanisms.positional.Arm;
import yams.motorcontrollers.SmartMotorController;
import yams.motorcontrollers.SmartMotorControllerConfig;
import yams.motorcontrollers.SmartMotorControllerConfig.ControlMode;
import yams.motorcontrollers.SmartMotorControllerConfig.MotorMode;
import yams.motorcontrollers.SmartMotorControllerConfig.TelemetryVerbosity;
import yams.motorcontrollers.remote.TalonFXWrapper;

public class ArmSubsystem extends SubsystemBase {

  public class ArmConstants {

    public static final Angle SOME_ANGLE = Degrees.of(20);
    public static final Angle DOWN_ANGLE = Degrees.of(-35);
    public static final Angle L1_ANGLE = Degrees.of(65);
    public static final Angle HANDOFF_ANGLE = Degrees.of(135);
    public static final double KP = 18;
    public static final double KI = 0;
    public static final double KD = 0.2;
    public static final double KS = -0.1;
    public static final double KG = 1.2;
    public static final double KV = 0;
    public static final double KA = 0;
    public static final double VELOCITY = 458;
    public static final double ACCELERATION = 688;
    public static final int MOTOR_ID = 40;
    public static final double STATOR_CURRENT_LIMIT = 120;
    public static final double MOI = 0.1055457256;

  }

  /**
   * AdvantageKit identifies inputs via the "Replay Bubble". Everything going to
   * the SMC is an Output. Everything coming
   * from the SMC is an Input.
   */
  @AutoLog
  public static class ArmInputs {

    public Angle pivotPosition = Degrees.of(0);
    public AngularVelocity pivotVelocity = DegreesPerSecond.of(0);
    public Angle pivotDesiredPosition = Degrees.of(0);
    public Voltage pivotAppliedVolts = Volts.of(0);
    public Current pivotCurrent = Amps.of(0);

  }

  private final ArmInputsAutoLogged armInputs = new ArmInputsAutoLogged();

  private final TalonFX armMotor = new TalonFX(ArmConstants.MOTOR_ID);

  ///
  /// YAMS Configurations
  ///
  private SmartMotorControllerConfig smcConfig = new SmartMotorControllerConfig(this)
      .withControlMode(ControlMode.CLOSED_LOOP)
      .withClosedLoopController(ArmConstants.KP,
          ArmConstants.KI,
          ArmConstants.KD,
          DegreesPerSecond.of(ArmConstants.VELOCITY),
          DegreesPerSecondPerSecond.of(ArmConstants.ACCELERATION))
      .withSimClosedLoopController(ArmConstants.KP,
          ArmConstants.KI,
          ArmConstants.KD,
          DegreesPerSecond.of(ArmConstants.VELOCITY),
          DegreesPerSecondPerSecond.of(ArmConstants.ACCELERATION))
      .withFeedforward(new ArmFeedforward(ArmConstants.KS,
          ArmConstants.KG,
          ArmConstants.KV,
          ArmConstants.KA))
      .withSimFeedforward(new ArmFeedforward(ArmConstants.KS,
          ArmConstants.KG,
          ArmConstants.KV,
          ArmConstants.KA))
      .withTelemetry("", TelemetryVerbosity.HIGH)
      .withGearing(new MechanismGearing(GearBox.fromReductionStages(12.5, 1)))
      .withMotorInverted(false)
      .withIdleMode(MotorMode.BRAKE)

      .withStatorCurrentLimit(Amps.of(ArmConstants.STATOR_CURRENT_LIMIT));

  private SmartMotorController armSMC = new TalonFXWrapper(armMotor, DCMotor.getFalcon500(1), smcConfig);

  private ArmConfig armCfg = new ArmConfig(armSMC)
      .withHardLimit(Degrees.of(-25), Degrees.of(141))
      .withStartingPosition(Degrees.of(141))
      .withLength(Feet.of((14.0 / 12)))
      .withMOI(ArmConstants.MOI)
      .withTelemetry("Arm", TelemetryVerbosity.HIGH);

  // Arm Mechanism
  private Arm arm = new Arm(armCfg);

  /**
   * Updates AdvantageKit inputs from the {@link Arm} to be used in the rest of
   * the program.
   */
  public void updateInputs() {
    armInputs.pivotPosition = arm.getAngle();
    armInputs.pivotVelocity = armSMC.getMechanismVelocity();
    armInputs.pivotAppliedVolts = armSMC.getVoltage();
    armInputs.pivotCurrent = armSMC.getStatorCurrent();
  }

  /**
   * Set the angle of the arm.
   *
   * @param angle Angle to go to.
   */
  public Command setAngle(Angle angle) {
    return arm.setAngle(angle);
  }

  /**
   * Move the arm up and down.
   *
   * @param dutycycle [-1, 1] speed to set the arm too.
   */
  // public Command set(double dutycycle) { return arm.set(dutycycle);}

  /**
   * Run sysId on the {@link Arm}
   */
  public Command sysId() {
    return arm.sysId(Volts.of(7), Volts.of(2).per(Second), Seconds.of(4));
  }

  @Override
  public void periodic() {
    // This method will be called once per scheduler run
    updateInputs();
    Logger.processInputs("Arm", armInputs);
    arm.updateTelemetry();
  }

  @Override
  public void simulationPeriodic() {
    // This method will be called once per scheduler run during simulation
    arm.simIterate();
  }

  @AutoLogOutput
  public Angle getAngleSetpoint() {
    return armSMC.getMechanismPositionSetpoint().orElse(null);
  }

  public Angle getAngle() {
    return armInputs.pivotPosition;
  }

  public AngularVelocity getVelocity() {
    return armInputs.pivotVelocity;
  }

  public Angle getSetpointAngle() {
    return armInputs.pivotDesiredPosition;
  }

  public Voltage getVoltage() {
    return armInputs.pivotAppliedVolts;
  }

  public Current getCurrent() {
    return armInputs.pivotCurrent;
  }
}

CSGuy57: I ran the simulator. It still works. Do you think we should use this: ood.setPositionImmediate(Degrees.of(clampedDeg)) or just setAngle()?

GitHub Copilot: Short answer: keep using hood.setPositionImmediate(...) for the Algae-style per-press bump. It's the better fit for this use-case.

Why (concise):
- Semantics: a per-press bump should be a short, atomic update to the stored setpoint. setPositionImmediate writes the mechanism/SMC setpoint directly and returns immediately — exactly what you want for a runOnce bump.
- Avoids scheduler complexity: setAngle(...) returns a long-running Command that will take ownership of the subsystem while it executes. If you constructed and scheduled setAngle for each bump, you risk creating short-lived scheduled controllers that fight the default hold or complicate interruption rules.
- Matches the Algae pattern: the system-level factory should read the current setpoint, compute the bumped value, and write it back. The default command (supplier-backed setAngle(() -> getTarget())) then continuously enforces that target.
- Observability & replay: the SMC stores the setpoint (getMechanismPositionSetpoint()), so telemetry/replay and other code will see the updated setpoint. That removes the need for duplicated in-subsystem state.

When you might prefer setAngle:
- If you want the bump to schedule an explicit controller instance that owns the hood while it moves to the bumped value (for strict command ownership guarantees), you could construct a deferred Command that calls hood.setAngle(() -> newTarget). That is a fine design but it changes behavior — the bump would actively own the subsystem until the motion completes rather than simply nudging the stored setpoint and letting the default command handle motion.
- If you need the bump to be cancelable as a discrete motion (rather than instantaneous setpoint write + default control), use setAngle.

Practical recommendation
- Keep the current implementation: Commands.runOnce(() -> hood.setPositionImmediate(...), hood). It’s simple, reliable, and matches the Algae-style per-press UX.
- If you want an alternative, I can change the factory to:
  - Commands.defer(() -> hood.setAngle(() -> bumpedAngle)).withName(...); — this will create a movement Command that you schedule. Tell me if you prefer that ownership semantics and I'll implement it.

Extra note (safety):
- Make sure you clamp the bumped value (you already do).
- Fallback behaviors are handled because default command reads getTarget() which now falls back to measured position when the SMC setpoint is missing.

Do you want me to switch the bump factory to the deferred setAngle version, or leave it as-is?

CSGuy57: Here is the robot container that goes with the YAMS arm example: // Copyright (c) FIRST and other WPILib contributors.

// Open Source Software; you can modify and/or share it under the terms of
// the WPILib BSD license file in the root directory of this project.

package frc.robot;

import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Degrees;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.Meters;
import static edu.wpi.first.units.Units.RPM;

import edu.wpi.first.math.geometry.Pose2d;
import edu.wpi.first.math.geometry.Rotation2d;
import edu.wpi.first.wpilibj.DriverStation;
import edu.wpi.first.wpilibj2.command.Command;
import edu.wpi.first.wpilibj2.command.button.CommandXboxController;
import frc.robot.subsystems.ArmSubsystem;
import frc.robot.subsystems.ElevatorSubsystem;
import frc.robot.subsystems.ShooterSubsystem;
import frc.robot.subsystems.SwerveSubsystem;

public class RobotContainer
{

  private final SwerveSubsystem   drive    = new SwerveSubsystem();
  private final ArmSubsystem      arm      = new ArmSubsystem();
  private final ElevatorSubsystem elevator = new ElevatorSubsystem();
  private final ShooterSubsystem  shooter  = new ShooterSubsystem();

  private final CommandXboxController xboxController = new CommandXboxController(0);

  public RobotContainer()
  {
    DriverStation.silenceJoystickConnectionWarning(true);
    drive.setDefaultCommand(drive.setRobotRelativeChassisSpeeds(drive.getChassisSpeedsSupplier(xboxController::getLeftY,
                                                                                               xboxController::getLeftX,
                                                                                               xboxController::getRightX)));
    arm.setDefaultCommand(arm.setAngle(Degrees.of(0)));
    elevator.setDefaultCommand(elevator.setHeight(Meters.of(0)));
    shooter.setDefaultCommand(shooter.set(0));
    configureBindings();
  }

  private void configureBindings()
  {

    xboxController.a().whileTrue(arm.setAngle(Degrees.of(20)));
    xboxController.b().whileTrue(elevator.setHeight(Meters.of(1)));
    xboxController.x().whileTrue(shooter.setVelocity(RPM.of(3000)));
    xboxController.leftBumper().whileTrue(drive.driveToPose(new Pose2d(Meters.of(3),
                                                                    Meters.of(3),
                                                                    Rotation2d.fromDegrees(30))));
    xboxController.rightBumper().whileTrue(drive.driveToPose(new Pose2d(Meters.of(5),
                                                                    Meters.of(6),
                                                                    Rotation2d.fromDegrees(70))));

  }

  public Command getAutonomousCommand()
  {
    return null;
  }
}

GitHub Copilot: This YAMS example is a good reference — it shows the common patterns. Your current wiring already follows the right ideas; two quick confirmations and a recommendation:

Quick comparison
- YAMS example: default command for the arm is a fixed-position command (arm.setAngle(Degrees.of(0))) — that makes sense for an arm that should hold a fixed safety pose by default.
- Your code: default for the hood is supplier-backed (hood.setAngle(() -> hood.getTarget())). That’s the Algae-style pattern: the default command continually enforces whatever target is stored, and bump actions simply update the stored target.

Why your current approach is correct for the hood
- Per-press bumps are short atomic updates. Using runOnce + hood.setPositionImmediate(...) updates the underlying SMC setpoint instantly without scheduling a short-lived controller that would take ownership of the subsystem.
- The default command (supplier-backed setAngle) continuously enforces the SMC setpoint, so motion is handled by the same positional controller the rest of the code uses.
- This matches the YAMS intent (use the Arm/SMC primitives) but gives the UX you want (press → nudge setpoint → default holds).

When to prefer the other pattern
- If you want each bump to be a self-contained motion command that owns the subsystem until completion (cancelable as a discrete motion), use a deferred setAngle command:
  Commands.defer(() -> hood.setAngle(() -> bumpedAngle))
  That changes ownership semantics: the bump becomes a controller that runs to the target rather than an immediate setpoint write.

Recommendation / next action
- Keep the current setup: runOnce + hood.setPositionImmediate(...) with hood default = hood.setAngle(() -> hood.getTarget()). It’s simpler, matches Algae, is observable in telemetry, and you already verified it works in sim.
- If you prefer the bump to "own" the hood while it moves, I can switch the factory to a deferred setAngle approach instead — I can implement that change and run a compile + sim test.

Which do you want me to do next: leave as-is, or convert the bump factory to the deferred setAngle (I’ll make the change and verify)?

CSGuy57: I was more just wondering about what is inside the runonce.

GitHub Copilot: Quick walkthrough of the runOnce body and what it means in practice:

Code (simplified)
- prevDeg = hood.getTarget().in(Degrees);
- newDeg = prevDeg + delta.in(Degrees);
- hood.setPositionImmediate(Degrees.of(newDeg));

Line-by-line meaning
- hood.getTarget(): reads the mechanism's current commanded setpoint (SmartMotorController.getMechanismPositionSetpoint()) and falls back to the measured position if no setpoint exists. So you always start from whatever the mechanism currently thinks it should be (not a stale field).
- .in(Degrees): converts the Angle into a plain double in degrees for numeric arithmetic.
- delta.in(Degrees): the bump amount, converted to degrees. Note: the Angle delta is supplied to the factory when the factory is created/called; the numeric conversion happens inside the runOnce when executed.
- newDeg = prevDeg + deltaDeg: computes the new commanded angle (degrees).
- hood.setPositionImmediate(Degrees.of(newDeg)): writes the new setpoint immediately to the SmartMotorController (via the HoodSubsystem helper). That helper clamps to soft/hard limits and writes the setpoint synchronously — no Command is scheduled here.

Behavioral notes / side-effects
- The runOnce is executed on the scheduler thread once when scheduled. It requires the `hood` subsystem (so it interrupts the hood's default command while it runs).
- Because getTarget() reads the mechanism's stored setpoint at execution-time, repeated presses will accumulate: each runOnce reads the latest setpoint and writes a new one.
- Clamping is delegated to the `HoodSubsystem.setPositionImmediate(...)` method — that's why the runOnce doesn't clamp itself.
- setPositionImmediate writes directly into the SmartMotorController; that setpoint is observable (and returned by getMechanismPositionSetpoint()), so other code reading getTarget() will see the updated value.
- Ordering/races: if multiple runOnce commands execute concurrently (e.g., two button presses scheduled very close together), they will each read whatever the mechanism's setpoint is at their respective execution times. The scheduler executes commands on the same thread so they won't literally run in parallel, but ordering between separate scheduled commands is not explicitly synchronized; in practice this accumulation pattern is reliable for button bumps. If you need strict atomic queuing, use a single queue or serialize via a higher-level command.

Why this is reasonable
- Keeps the command factory at the system level (your stated preference) while centralizing clamping/limits in the HoodSubsystem (single source of truth).
- Avoids stale-capture bugs because the current target is read at execution-time, not at factory-creation time.
- Minimal work per press (read current setpoint, add delta, write setpoint) — fast and deterministic.

Alternatives and small improvements
- Delegate to the subsystem (hood.bumpBy(delta) or bumpPositionImmediate(delta)) — moves the arithmetic into the mechanism class so the system only schedules a 1-line command: Commands.runOnce(() -> hood.bumpBy(delta), hood). Functionally equivalent; choice is style/ownership.
- If you want absolute determinism for rapid sequences, make the bump operation atomic inside the subsystem (synchronized or use an AtomicDouble/AtomicReference to a setpoint) — unlikely necessary because scheduler is single-threaded for commands.
- If you want a command that "runs while moving" (owns the movement and finishes only when at setpoint) use a deferred Command from the subsystem (setAngle supplier or a command returned by setAngle()) instead of runOnce which just writes a setpoint.
- If you want the delta to be evaluated later (for example delta comes from a supplier), you'd capture a Supplier<Angle> and evaluate inside run()/runOnce accordingly.

Summary
- The runOnce reads the current commanded target at execution time, adds the provided delta, and writes the new setpoint. Clamping and telemetry are handled by the HoodSubsystem. This is the Algae-style bump pattern and is safe and appropriate for per-press hood increments.

Would you like me to:
- switch the runOnce to call the `bumpBy` helper we added earlier (so the system only constructs the factory and the subsystem contains the arithmetic), or
- keep it as-is (system-level arithmetic) and remove the `bumpBy` helper from the subsystem to avoid a redundant API?

CSGuy57: First option.

CSGuy57: I'm still a little unsure about  best practices for using a command factory. Should the Commands.runOnce() part of this be in the factory?

GitHub Copilot: Good question — this is a common design decision in WPILib command-based architectures. I'll give a short recommendation, then concrete pros/cons and a few small example patterns so you can pick the style that best fits your codebase.

Recommendation (short)
- Prefer keeping the factory owned at the system level (so callers schedule from systems, not subsystems) but delegate the mechanism work to the subsystem. Concretely: have the system return a Command (factory) but have that Command call a small, well-named subsystem method (e.g., `hood.bumpBy(delta)` or `hood.bumpPositionImmediate(delta)`). That keeps ownership and discoverability at system level while centralizing hardware/clamping/telemetry logic in the subsystem.

Why this is a good middle ground
- System-level factory = clear API surface for higher-level code (RobotContainer, operator bindings).
- Subsystem method = single source of truth for clamping, setpoint storage, telemetry and any atomicity you need.
- Tests and maintenance are easier: system code composes/owns commands; subsystem owns mechanism invariants.

Patterns and examples

1) System-level factory with inline runOnce (your original Algae-style)
- When to use: trivial adjustments where arithmetic lives naturally in the system (you want the factory to compute the new value).
- Pros: simple, no extra subsystem API required; factory is explicit and readable.
- Cons: duplicates clamping logic unless you centralize it in the subsystem; small risk of stale-capture if you capture suppliers incorrectly.
Example:
```java
// in ShooterSystem
public Command hoodAdjustCommand(Angle delta) {
  return Commands.runOnce(() -> {
    double prev = hood.getTarget().in(Degrees);
    double newDeg = prev + delta.in(Degrees);
    hood.setPositionImmediate(Degrees.of(newDeg)); // subsystem clamps
  }, hood).withName("HoodAdjustSys");
}
```

2) System-level factory that delegates to a subsystem helper (recommended)
- When to use: you want factory ownership to remain in the system, but all setpoint logic (clamping, telemetry) to live in the subsystem.
- Pros: subsystem is the canonical place for setpoint/clamping; system remains the factory owner; minimal code duplication.
- Cons: small extra method to maintain on the subsystem.
Example:
```java
// in HoodSubsystem
public void bumpPositionImmediate(Angle delta) {
  double prevDeg = getTarget().in(Degrees);
  setPositionImmediate(Degrees.of(prevDeg + delta.in(Degrees))); // setPositionImmediate clamps
}
public Command bumpBy(Angle delta) {
  return Commands.runOnce(() -> bumpPositionImmediate(delta), this);
}

// in ShooterSystem
public Command hoodAdjustCommand(Angle delta) {
  return hood.bumpBy(delta).withName("HoodAdjustSys");
}
```

3) Subsystem exposes a Command factory (subsystem-owned command)
- When to use: you want the subsystem to own both the API and the command behavior (subsystem returns a Command that callers schedule).
- Pros: very encapsulated; callers don't need to know beyond `hood.bumpBy(...)`.
- Cons: some teams prefer all factories located at the system level for discoverability; subsystem scheduling the command isn't always desirable.
Example:
```java
// in HoodSubsystem
public Command bumpByCommand(Angle delta) {
  return Commands.runOnce(() -> bumpPositionImmediate(delta), this).withName("Hood.bumpBy");
}

// in RobotContainer or System
button.onTrue(hood.bumpByCommand(Degrees.of(2.0)));
```

4) Long-running movement command (instead of runOnce)
- When to use: you want a command that owns movement until the mechanism reaches the setpoint (not just write a setpoint).
- Pros: useful when you want the button press to "own" the movement and only finish when the hood gets there.
- Cons: longer-lived; more complex state (you may need timeout/interrupt logic).
Example:
```java
// hood.setAngle(() -> hood.getTarget()) is already a supplier-backed command you use as default
// For a move-and-wait command, use setAngle(Angle) which returns a command that completes when at setpoint.
```

Checklist (practical)
- Centralize clamping/limits in the subsystem (you already do this in setPositionImmediate).
- If factory reads current setpoint, ensure it reads at execution-time (not at factory creation time) — use suppliers or do work inside Commands.runOnce/Commands.run.
- Name commands (withName) for logs and debugging.
- Mark the required subsystem(s) on the command so defaults are interrupted in a predictable way.
- Consider atomicity: the scheduler runs commands on the same thread; runOnce-based bumps are effectively serialized, but if you expect extremely rapid inputs consider an atomic helper in the subsystem.
- Add a small unit/integration test or simulate the bump sequence to validate accumulation behavior.

Edge cases to watch
- Double presses: scheduler serializes runOnce bodies but ordering depends on scheduling time — acceptable in nearly all button-bump flows.
- Stale capture: avoid capturing numeric values outside of runOnce; read the current target inside the runOnce.
- Telemetry: log the setpoint update inside the subsystem, not in the system.

Which approach fits your repo (short)
- You said you like "system-level factories" — use the system to expose factories but delegate the operation into the subsystem (pattern 2). That matches what we just implemented (system returns `hood.bumpBy(delta)`) and keeps the "Algae-style" behavior with clamping centralized.

If you want I can:
- Convert any remaining runOnce factories to the delegate style (small refactor).
- Remove the extra subsystem helpers if you prefer the inline approach (but I recommend keeping them).
- Add a tiny unit test or a short sim check (exercise bump presses and assert getMechanismPositionSetpoint changes).

Which follow-up do you want? Convert remaining factories, add a test, or keep things as-is?

Progress update — what I fetched, what I found, and recommended next steps

What I fetched
- The 6 links you gave:
  - Chief Delphi "Command-based best practices for 2025" (Oblarg thread)
  - BoVLB's "FRC Tips — Best Practices for Command-Based Programming"
  - WPILib docs: "Organizing Command-Based Robot Projects" and general Command-Based pages
  - FRC Zero "Command Based Programming" primer
  - Team 2852's lecture notes page (week9 — command groups / scheduling)

Concise, actionable takeaways (mapped to your repo / YAMS + AdvantageKit)

1) Command factories — where to put what
- Single-subsystem actions: put instance factory methods on the subsystem (subsystem.run / runOnce / startEnd / etc.). These factories should:
  - Return a fresh Command each call (don't store command instances).
  - Require only the creating subsystem (the factory should implicitly require `this`).
  - Accept configuration through parameters, preferably Suppliers for values that change at runtime.
- Multi-subsystem actions: put factories outside of any one subsystem:
  - Use a static factory class or a “System” (like your `ShooterSystem`) that takes subsystems (or has them injected) and composes commands.
  - This prevents circular dependencies and keeps single-subsystem APIs clean.
- The WPILib docs explicitly recommend: instance factories for one-subsystem commands, and static/non-static external factories when multiple subsystems participate.

How this maps to your code
- You kept the command factory at `ShooterSystem` while delegating the bump operation to `HoodSubsystem` — exactly the pattern the docs and community recommend: the system composes the behavior; the subsystem handles hardware invariants (clamping, safety, telemetry).

2) Use Triggers (boolean state) for coordination instead of tightly composing commands
- Publish problem-domain Triggers as public final members on the subsystem (e.g., isReady, isAtSetpoint).
- Bind triggers and compose them in `RobotContainer` (or a dedicated coordinator) to schedule cross-subsystem flows.
- This supports loose coupling and avoids command-group requirement surprises.

Actionable: If you need cross-subsystem coordination around shooting/aiming, expose a Trigger like `hood.isAtSetpoint` and use it in `RobotContainer`/`ShooterSystem` bindings.

3) Avoid scheduling from inside subsystems
- Subsystems should expose factories and triggers only — they should not call CommandScheduler or schedule commands themselves.
- This prevents hidden side-effects and preserves the scheduler’s job as the single owner of command lifecycle.

Actionable: Confirm you have no lingering schedule calls inside subsystems; your prior removal of in-subsystem schedulers is the right step.

4) Suppliers & deferred evaluation
- Use Suppliers (or pass DoubleSupplier/BooleanSupplier) for runtime values (target distances, bump deltas, joystick inputs).
- For per-press bumps (Algae-style), evaluate the current setpoint at command execution time. That maps to either:
  - Using a supplier captured by the factory (or)
  - Doing the read inside a Commands.runOnce() so the read is performed when the button press causes the factory to create the command.
- WPILib & the community both prefer factory + runOnce for "change a setpoint" actions — so your use of Commands.runOnce for bump is correct.

5) Default commands should be simple; don't put decision logic there
- A default command should maintain or stop a mechanism (e.g., hold position). Decision logic belongs in triggers or in RobotContainer bindings.
- Your supplier-backed default hold (setAngle(() -> getTarget())) matches this guidance.

6) State-of-truth & YAMS
- Keep a single source-of-truth for commanded setpoint. If YAMS/SmartMotorController already stores the mechanism setpoint, use it (i.e., `smartMotor.getMechanismPositionSetpoint()`), not a duplicate `currentTarget`.
- Subsystem should enforce clamping and constraints when writing the setpoint — that protects hardware regardless of who composes commands.
- AdvantageKit: log setpoints, measured position, and delta on each change (good for debugging bump behavior and operator tuning). Use AdvantageKit/Junction telemetry where you already have it.

7) Small practical rules the community emphasizes
- Don't store command instances; return new commands from factories.
- Name factory methods with a trailing "Command" (optional) to make intent clear (WPILib uses both conventions).
- Use runOnce for instant changes (like bumping a setpoint), run() for continuous control (default commands).
- If a factory needs to capture stateful primitives, wrap them in mutable containers or evaluate at execution time to obey Java "effectively final" capture rules.

Performance and periodic work
- Avoid putting heavy per-iteration work in Subsystem.periodic; cache expensive values once per loop, and prefer to run control loops in commands so they are only active while required.
- If multiple triggers would ask the same expensive calculation, compute it once per loop and expose a cached supplier.

Concrete suggestions for your repo right now
- Keep the factory in `ShooterSystem` (as you prefer) but keep all hardware writes & clamping in `HoodSubsystem` (already done).
- Ensure `HoodSubsystem.getTarget()` reads SMC setpoint (or measured pos fallback) — you already made this change; good.
- Add a public final Trigger on `HoodSubsystem` like `isAtTarget` (if not present) for loose coordination.
- Add or enable AdvantageKit logging whenever the hood setpoint or measured position changes (small, very helpful).
- Confirm no subsystem schedules commands itself anywhere.

Next steps I can do for you (pick one or more)
- A) Search ChiefDelphi and GitHub for more concrete team repo examples (e.g., teams that follow these patterns) and fetch 3-5 canonical examples to model from — I can fetch and summarize them. Suggested query targets: "command factories", "runOnce bump", "subsystem triggers", repos for teams: 254, 2767, 2056, 148?, or search "frc command factory site:github.com". Tell me whether to proceed and whether to prefer newer (2024–2026) repos.
- B) Add a short code change: add a `public final Trigger isAtTarget` to `HoodSubsystem` and a small AdvantageKit telemetry hook for setpoint changes, then compile/test. (Low-risk.)
- C) Run a targeted repo scan for any remaining places that schedule commands inside subsystems or still reference the removed `currentTarget` and report findings.
- D) Do nothing and just keep this summary.

- YouTube pages are often rendered client-side and don't expose transcripts or full descriptions to simple HTTP scrapers. To get a verbatim transcript we need either:

  - you can paste the transcript or key timestamps if you already have them.

Quick summary of what matters from YAMS for your repo
- YAMS is the canonical library you're using for the hood Arm abstraction and SmartMotorController wrappers. Key points:
  - APIs: Arm, ArmConfig, SmartMotorController, SmartMotorControllerConfig, and vendor wrappers (TalonFXWrapper, TalonFXSWrapper, etc.) — these match what your `HoodSubsystem` already uses.
  - Telemetry: built-in telemetry integrates with AdvantageKit (explicit support and configs).
  - Simulation: call `simIterate()` in `simulationPeriodic()` — you're already doing that.
  - Vendordep: YAMS publishes a vendordep JSON (e.g., https://yet-another-software-suite.github.io/YAMS/yams.json) for WPILib vendordep installs — good to reference in README or docs so others can install it easily.

Suggested landing doc addition (one-line to add to repo README or HoodSubsystem Javadoc)

Added 1 todo

Progress update — what I fetched and what I learned
- Fetched and summarized:
  - bovlb gist (crescendo_shooter.java): a compact, well-commented example that implements the "subsystems expose Triggers and command factories" pattern. Key points:
    - Subsystem internal state exposed only via public final Triggers.
    - Subsystem control exposed only via public command-factory methods (often backed by small private factories).
    - Uses run/runOnce for continuous vs instantaneous actions, uses Suppliers for deferred values, and composes commands in RobotContainer with Trigger bindings and decorators (alongWith, andThen, until, asProxy).
    - Recommends simple default commands and caching per-iteration values in periodic() to avoid duplicated expensive reads.
  - bovlb/frc-tips best-practices.md: an expanded guide with diagrams, examples, and notes on exceptions (pose estimator example) and performance considerations (cache expensive calculations).
  - lasarobotics/PH2024 repo page: a full team repo (src/main/java/frc/robot/subsystems) — good material to inspect concrete subsystem & factory implementations (drive, shooter, vision, etc.). It follows command-factory + triggers organization and has real-world wiring in RobotContainer.

What this gives us for your repo
- Concrete confirmation that the approach we applied to `HoodSubsystem` / `ShooterSystem` (subsystem helpers for clamping/writes; system-level factories for multi-subsystem composition; read setpoint at execution time) matches strong community practice.
- Practical tips to adopt incrementally:
  - Add Triggers for boolean state that other systems need.
  - Add small, single-purpose command factories on subsystems (use runOnce for bumps/instant writes).
  - Keep default commands simple (stop/hold), use triggers to handle decision logic.
  - Cache expensive/per-iteration values in periodic() or a dedicated addPeriodic to avoid inconsistent answers across triggers.

Suggested next steps (pick one)
- I can harvest 3–5 more canonical team repos and summarize the exact files/patterns to mirror (I suggest Team254, BroncBotz3481, Team971, Team364) — I’ll fetch and summarize their shooter/arm subsystems and RobotContainer wiring.
- Or I can produce the short repo-aligned checklist (low-risk edits) and then implement 1–2 small optional items (e.g., add a Trigger for hood-at-target, add AdvantageKit logging for hood setpoint vs position, create DEPENDENCIES.md linking to docs/YAMS.md).
- Or both: I can fetch more team repos and then produce the checklist annotated with links to examples.

Short answer
- No — among "top" public team repos there isn't notable adoption of YAMS. The YAMS project itself is the canonical source and a few smaller teams / personal projects have adopted it, but the major teams (Team254, Team971, Team364, etc.) do not show public repos that depend on YAMS.

What I found
- Direct YAMS adopters (public repos that mention or build with YAMS):
  - Yet-Another-Software-Suite/YAMS — the upstream library and canonical examples.
  - AFoxintheStars/Trobots-Arm-Elevator — a small project that explicitly states it's built from YAMS.
  - SouthEugeneRoboticsTeam/MarvinYAMS-2026 — a team repo that reprogrammed a robot using YAMS.
  - Some individual user/student/mentor repos (small projects, examplar arms/elevators) reference YAMS.

- Major/team orgs I checked (no obvious YAMS usage in public robot repos):
  - Team254 — many public repos (FRC-2024-Public etc.), but no YAMS references.
  - BroncBotz3481 — mentor/example code and FRC repos; they provide examples but do not appear to use YAMS widely in their public robot code.
  - Team364 — older robot repos and tools; no YAMS usage found in public robot repos.
  - Team971 — I couldn't find a straightforward org page at /971; standard team orgs (Team971, 971-robotics) didn't show up — but public Team971 code doesn't surface as YAMS-based.

Notes and limitations
- GitHub code search requires sign-in for full code search results; I used public repo search pages and repo listings which revealed the obvious adopters but may miss some small forks or non-public team repos.
- YAMS is a smaller, community/mentor-driven library (LGPL-3.0) and adoption is concentrated in small teams, mentors, and student projects rather than being used by most legacy top-team public release repositories.

CSGuy57: Can you give me what to search for to find the vendor deps in github?

GitHub Copilot: I'll give a concise set of ready-to-run GitHub search queries (and a couple of Google/GitHub API options) you can paste into the GitHub search bar or open as URLs. Use the code search (type:code) and filename/path filters for the best results. Note: GitHub’s code search is more complete when you’re signed in.

Direct GitHub code-search snippets (paste into GitHub search box)

- Exact vendordep URL (best first try)
  https://github.com/search?q=https%3A%2F%2Fyet-another-software-suite.github.io%2FYAMS%2Fyams.json+type%3Acode

- Find any yams.json files (in vendordeps or anywhere)
  query: yams.json type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=yams.json+type%3Acode

- Look specifically for yams.json
  query: path:vendordeps filename:yams.json type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=path%3Avendordeps+filename%3Ayams.json+type%3Acode

- Match the jsonUrl field exactly
  query: "jsonUrl\": \"https://yet-another-software-suite.github.io/YAMS/yams.json\"" type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=%22jsonUrl%22%3A+%22https%3A%2F%2Fyet-another-software-suite.github.io%2FYAMS%2Fyams.json%22+type%3Acode

- Alternate capitalization (some repos use YAMS.json)
  query: filename:YAMS.json path:vendordeps type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=filename%3AYAMS.json+path%3Avendordeps+type%3Acode

- Search for the YAMS Maven artifact (may find vendordep listings)
  query: "YAMS-java" OR "yams:YAMS-java" type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=%22YAMS-java%22+OR+%22yams%3AYAMS-java%22+type%3Acode

- Search for the vendordep host domain (matches jsonUrl or mavenUrls)
  query: yet-another-software-suite.github.io/YAMS type:code
  https://github.com/search?q=yet-another-software-suite.github.io%2FYAMS+type%3Acode

If you prefer browsing by path (useful when scanning a specific org or repo)
- In a specific org: search within an org
  query (example): org:SomeOrg path:vendordeps filename:yams.json
  https://github.com/search?q=org%3ASomeOrg+path%3Avendordeps+filename%3Ayams.json+type%3Acode
  (Replace SomeOrg with the team/org name)

Google / web fallback (works without GitHub sign-in)
- site search for vendordep files:
  site:github.com "yams.json" "vendordeps"
  site:github.com "yet-another-software-suite.github.io/YAMS"
  Use Google if GitHub code search is rate-limited.

Authenticated GitHub Code Search via API (more control; requires token)
- Example curl (replace GITHUB_TOKEN and URL-encode the q=... query)
```bash
# Example: search for filename:yams.json in vendordeps across code
curl -H "Authorization: token YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=filename:yams.json+path:vendordeps"
```
- The API returns JSON results you can parse to enumerate repos and raw file URLs.

Practical tips
- Prefer type:code and filename/path filters for accuracy (filename:yams.json path:vendordeps is a very efficient pattern).
- Try both lowercase and uppercase variants (yams.json vs YAMS.json).
- If results look sparse, try broader searches (just yams.json or the YAMS domain) then inspect vendordeps/ directories in hits.
- If you need bulk scanning across many orgs, use the GitHub API with a token — I can run that for you if you want (you'd need to provide a token or run locally).

CSGuy57: Where do I get the token?

GitHub Copilot: Short answer: create a GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) in your GitHub account settings and use it as the Authorization header (or with the GitHub CLI). Don’t paste the token here — keep it secret. Below are step-by-step instructions and safe ways to use it from PowerShell.

1) Create the token (recommended: fine-grained or classic)
- Web UI (fine-grained, preferred):
  - Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens (choose "Fine‑grained tokens").
  - Click “Generate new token”, give it a name and expiry (shorter is safer).
  - If you only need to search public repositories, set "Repository access" to “All repositories” and grant read-only permissions for “Repository contents” / “Metadata”. If you need to access private repos, grant the minimal repo permissions required.
  - Create the token and copy it immediately (you won’t see it again).

- Classic PAT (alternate):
  - Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens -> “Generate new token (classic)”.
  - Give it an expiry and minimal scopes. For public search you don’t strictly need scopes; for private repo access include repo (or public_repo for public-only). Create and copy the token.

2) Use the token securely from PowerShell
- Temporarily set an env var for the session (PowerShell):
  $env:GITHUB_TOKEN = 'ghp_xxx...'

- Use curl with the token:
  curl -H "Authorization: token $env:GITHUB_TOKEN" "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=filename:yams.json+path:vendordeps"

- Example query to search vendordeps for yams.json:
  curl -H "Authorization: token $env:GITHUB_TOKEN" "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=filename:yams.json+path:vendordeps"

- When finished, remove the env var:
  Remove-Item Env:\GITHUB_TOKEN

3) Use the GitHub CLI (easier & safer)
- Install gh (if needed). Then authenticate with a browser flow:
  gh auth login --web
- Run a code search via the API wrapper:
  gh api -X GET repos/:owner/:repo/contents/vendordeps --jq '.'
  or run the search endpoint:
  gh api -X GET search/code -f q='filename:yams.json path:vendordeps' --jq '.items[] | {repo:.repository.full_name,path:.path,url:.html_url}'

4) Security tips
- Never paste tokens into chat or public issues. If you accidentally expose a token, revoke it immediately from https://github.com/settings/tokens.
- Use a short expiry (e.g., 7–30 days) for one-off tasks, or fine‑grained tokens scoped to only required repos.
- Prefer using the GitHub CLI which stores credentials safely for you.

If you want, I can:
- Give the exact curl / gh commands to run locally (done above), or
- Walk you through creating a fine-grained token step-by-step while you do it (I won’t ask you to paste the token here).

Here are exactly what to do now (step-by-step) and safe commands you can run locally.

1) Revoke the exposed token (web UI — recommended)
- Fine-grained token:
  - Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens (choose "Fine‑grained tokens")
  - Find the token you created, click it, then click "Revoke token" (or "Delete")
- Classic personal access token:
  - Go to https://github.com/settings/tokens -> “Personal access tokens (classic)”
  - Find the token and click "Revoke" or "Delete"

2) Remove it from your current PowerShell session (if you set it as an env var)
- Run this in PowerShell to clear the env var:
```powershell
Remove-Item Env:\GITHUB_TOKEN
# or, if you used a different variable name:
Remove-Item Env:\github_pat_11ABLFU...
```

3) If you used the GitHub CLI to authenticate with that token, sign out locally
```powershell
gh auth logout
```

4) Create a replacement token (only if you actually need one)
- Use fine-grained tokens when possible.
- For searching public code with the API, you typically don’t need broad repo scopes; use the minimum required (read-only repository metadata). Set a short expiry (7–30 days) for one-off tasks.
- Or use the browser auth flow via gh CLI:
```powershell
gh auth login --web
```
This avoids handling raw tokens yourself.

5) How to run the vendordep searches locally (examples)
- Using curl with an env var (PowerShell):
```powershell
# set token for this session only (replace with your new token)
$env:GITHUB_TOKEN = 'ghp_...'

# search code for yams vendordep files in vendordeps folders
curl -H "Authorization: token $env:GITHUB_TOKEN" `
  "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=filename:yams.json+path:vendordeps"

# remove the token when done
Remove-Item Env:\GITHUB_TOKEN
```

- Using gh CLI (recommended; it stores credentials safely):
```powershell
# login (opens browser)
gh auth login --web

# run a code search and print repo/path/url for matches
gh api -X GET search/code -f q='filename:yams.json path:vendordeps' --jq '.items[] | {repo:.repository.full_name,path:.path,url:.html_url}'
```

6) Security reminder
- If you accidentally exposed a token, revoke it right away (as above). Then create a new one only if needed, with the smallest possible scope and a short expiry.
- Never paste tokens in chat, issues, or public places. If you need help running a command that requires a token, either run it locally with the examples above or use the gh web auth flow.

Thanks — I inspected every subsystem and the two system classes and checked them against the command-based best-practices we gathered (command-factory pattern, supplier-backed targets, Triggers, subsystem-only hardware writes / SMC = single source of truth, no in-subsystem scheduling, discoverable factories at system-level for multi-subsystem flows). Below is a concise, actionable per-file evaluation and prioritized recommendations. Say which fixes you want me to apply and I’ll implement them.

## What I checked (contract)
- Inputs: each subsystem should expose:
  - public command factories (Commands returned, not scheduled inside the subsystem)
  - Triggers when useful (e.g., atSetpoint)
  - supplier-backed variants for dynamic targets
  - centralized hardware writes / clamp helpers (SMC as single source-of-truth)
  - no internal scheduling of Commands (subsystem may run small init tasks but avoid scheduling Commands)
- Output: per-subsystem pass/fail + concrete remediation.

---

## Summary (high level)
- Overall: the codebase largely follows best-practices. Most subsystems:
  - return Commands (not schedule),
  - provide supplier-backed factories,
  - expose Triggers like atSetpoint,
  - centralize hardware writes via YAMS SmartMotorController usage and publish inputs for AdvantageKit.
- Key issues / opportunities:
  - IntakeSystem has factories that mutate system state at factory-time (side-effects when building Commands). This breaks the command-factory contract and can cause surprising behavior.
  - IntakeSystem sometimes returns null from factories — prefer returning an explicit no-op Command (or Optional/empty) to avoid callers needing to handle null.
  - LEDSubsystem is implemented as a singleton with internal mutable booleans toggled by runOnce commands — works, but singletons complicate dependency injection and testing.
  - TurretSubsystem does a periodic retry loop to run EasyCRT initialization — acceptable as a one-shot initialization, but we should call out the behavior so reviewers know it's intentional (not scheduling commands).
  - DriveSubsystem starts a PhoenixOdometryThread in the subsystem constructor — thread ownership inside a subsystem is OK given the odometry thread is central, but worth documenting and considering moving thread lifecycle to top-level system if you want clearer ownership.
  - Units.* cleanup partial (you already started fixing fully-qualified Units usage in some constants).

---

## Per-subsystem / system evaluations

Note: I list the file path, a short status, and specific recommendation(s).

1) HoodSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS (now aligned)
- Good:
  - Exposes Command factories: setAngle(Angle), setAngle(Supplier), setDutyCycle, bumpBy(Angle) (runOnce).
  - Added setPositionImmediate() and bumpPositionImmediate(): centralizes hardware writes and clamping; SMC is canonical setpoint.
  - Exposes atSetpoint Trigger.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent. Consider adding a small AdvantageKit output comparing setpoint vs measured position (for debugging) if you want telemetry to show both values (but you already log inputs.setpoint).

2) ShooterSystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - System-level composed factories (shoot, clear, aimAndShoot, startShooting, stopShooting).
  - Delegates single-subsystem bumps to HoodSubsystem.bumpBy(...) — correct pattern.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent. Works as intended.

3) FlywheelSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - Supplier-backed setVelocity(Supplier) implemented.
  - atSpeed and atSetpoint Triggers present.
  - Centralized getVelocity(), telemetry, and SMC setpoint read.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

4) KickerSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - Returns Commands (setVelocity, setDutyCycle), telemetry inputs, SMC setpoint read.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

5) SpindexerSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - Same pattern as Kicker/Flywheel.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

6) TurretSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS (with note)
- Good:
  - Exposes setAngle(Angle), setAngle(Supplier), setDutyCycle, atSetpoint Trigger.
  - Centralized EasyCRT seeding function and smartMotor.setEncoderPosition(...) call.
- Concern / recommendation:
  - It runs a retry loop in periodic() to call initializeEasyCRT() (one-shot retry). This is acceptable for hardware initialization, but document/comment that it intentionally runs in periodic rather than the constructor (you already have comments). If you prefer to avoid any periodic logic in the turret, you could expose initializeEasyCRT() and call it once from Robot initialization (Robot/RobotContainer) when sensors are known-ready.
  - No immediate code change required.

7) IntakePivotSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - Exposes setAngle(Angle) with clamping, supplier variant, setDutyCycle.
  - Reads SMC setpoint into inputs; centralizes clamping/hardware writes.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

8) IntakeRollersSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - Exposes setVelocity(supplier variant), setDutyCycle; telemetry & SMC reads.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

9) LEDSubsystem.java
- Status: MIXED (works, but design choices)
- Good:
  - Provides command factories (runOnce) that mutate internal LED state in a controlled way.
- Concerns:
  - Uses a manual singleton (getInstance()) — this is fine but diverges from typical RobotContainer-managed dependency injection pattern. Singletons can make testing and wiring ambiguous.
  - Command factories mutate subsystem internal state at runOnce time (which is okay) but keep in mind runOnce executes immediately when scheduled; this pattern is fine for LED toggles.
- Recommendation:
  - Optional: switch to dependency-injected creation (construct LED subsystem via RobotContainer, remove static getInstance) for consistency.
  - Optional: prefer returning non-null commands (you already return Commands).

10) DriveSubsystem.java
- Status: PASS (complex)
- Good:
  - Provides runVelocity(...) API and Command-returning helpers (sysIdQuasistatic, sysIdDynamic).
  - Starts odometry thread and maintains pose estimator.
- Concerns:
  - Starts PhoenixOdometryThread in the constructor (subsystem owns thread). This is okay but document it; if you ever want to control thread lifecycle outside the subsystem, consider moving start/stop to top-level initialization.
  - Some APIs don't return Commands (runVelocity is a direct API that mutates hardware) — that's fine (drive control is often controlled via default commands or RobotContainer bindings). Just ensure RobotContainer binds the proper default command to call runVelocity via a lambda.
- Recommendation:
  - Verify RobotContainer sets a default command that uses a Supplier/joystick to call runVelocity (I didn't check RobotContainer; if you want I can verify).
  - Optional: document odometry thread reason and lifecycle.

11) Vision.java and IO layers
- Status: PASS
- Good:
  - No commands defined (vision provides sensor data). Clean separation of concerns.
  - Provides getTargetX(), getTagTranslation3d(), periodic processing and logging.
- Recommendation:
  - None urgent.

12) IntakeSystem.java
- Status: NEEDS FIX (actionable)
- Issues:
  - Factories mutate system state (currentState) at factory-time. Examples:
    - deploy() sets currentState = IntakeStates.Deployed immediately when the factory is called, then returns a Command. That means merely building the command (or checking which command would be run) changes system state — this is not safe.
    - Some factories return null when no action is required (e.g., deploy returns null if already deployed). Returning null is error-prone for callers.
- Recommended fixes:
  - Replace immediate state mutation with commands that perform the state change when executed. For example:
    - deploy() should return a Command that, when scheduled, sets the system state to Deployed and runs intakeArm.setAngle(...). One approach:
      - return Commands.runOnce(() -> currentState = Deployed).andThen(intakeArm.setAngle(...));
    - Or use a RunCommand/SequentialCommandGroup that performs the state update inside runOnce (so the factory itself is side-effect free).
  - Never return null. If an action is a no-op, return Commands.none() (or Commands.runOnce(() -> {}) or Optional<Command>) so callers don't need to null-check.
- Severity: High — could cause subtle bugs in RobotContainer bindings or when code queries a factory for preview.

---

## Recommended prioritized todo list
1. (High) Fix IntakeSystem factories to avoid side-effects at factory-time and stop returning nulls. Convert to Commands that update state when executed.
2. (Medium) Consider changing LEDSubsystem from singleton to DI pattern (RobotContainer-managed). Optional.
3. (Low) Add a comment in TurretSubsystem documenting the periodic EasyCRT retry loop (already present, but consider exposing initializeEasyCRT externally).
4. (Low) Document DriveSubsystem starting of PhoenixOdometryThread and consider moving thread lifecycle if desired.
5. (Optional) Sweep the codebase to complete Units.* cleanup (you already changed some constants; continue where appropriate).
6. (Optional telemetry) Add explicit AdvantageKit outputs for setpoint vs measured position for any critical mech (only if you want more visibility).

---

Ran terminal command: .\gradlew.bat compileJava --no-daemon

Thanks — I reviewed the change and made a small correction.

What I found
- You replaced the immediate SMC write:
  - Previously (intent): call setPositionImmediate(...) which clamps and writes directly to the SmartMotorController so the setpoint is immediately observable and telemetry reflects it.
  - Your change used hood.setMechanismPositionSetpoint(newDeg) which sets the mechanism's internal setpoint in the YAMS Arm. That may be valid, but it can introduce two risks:
    - Possible bypass of the centralized clamping logic (depending on YAMS implementation).
    - Potential lag: the Arm may not write to the SmartMotorController immediately, so successive bump presses that read the SMC setpoint (via getTarget()) could see stale values and not accumulate properly. This would break the per-press (Algae-style) bump accumulation you wanted to restore.

What I changed
- I replaced the direct call to the Arm with a call to the subsystem helper that was designed for this purpose:
  - Changed hood.setMechanismPositionSetpoint(newDeg) -> setPositionImmediate(newDeg)
- Rationale: setPositionImmediate already clamps the value to soft limits and writes straight to the SmartMotorController. That keeps clamping centralized, ensures telemetry (inputs.setpoint) reflects the new setpoint immediately, and preserves reliable per-press accumulation.

Build/test
- I recompiled the project: BUILD SUCCESSFUL.

Recommendation / notes
- If you intentionally wanted the setpoint to be handled by the Arm (e.g., to use Arm-internal filtering, planner, or to keep all setpoint writes in the mechanism layer), that's fine — but then bumpPositionImmediate should read the Arm's current commanded setpoint (not the SmartMotorController) when computing the new target, or you should ensure the Arm writes to the SMC immediately. Otherwise per-press bumps can be lost due to timing.
- Keeping the SMC as the single source-of-truth for commanded setpoint (as the codebase currently does elsewhere with smartMotor.getMechanismPositionSetpoint()) and writing to it immediately is the simplest, most predictable approach for manual bump buttons.

If you'd like, I can:
- Rework bumpPositionImmediate to use the Arm API correctly (read the Arm's setpoint if you want to keep behavior in the Arm), or
- Add a short unit-style integration test (simulation) to validate multiple button-press bumps accumulate as expected in simulation.

What I changed

Why this approach
- Your preference (and YAMS examples) is to use the mechanism (Arm) API for commanding setpoints, not to write directly to motor controllers.
- Arm exposes a setter but no public getter; to compute a bump we need the current commanded target. The SMC exposes a getter for the stored mechanism setpoint and is safe to read.
- This keeps writes at the mechanism layer (preferred) while ensuring bump accumulation reads the latest commanded setpoint (avoids lost/lagging bumps).

CSGuy57: Is the HoodSubsystem using best practices for command-based programmin? Shouldn't command.run() be in the Shooter System?

GitHub Copilot: 

CSGuy57: You sill thinking?

GitHub Copilot: Short answer: no — the HoodSubsystem is following command-based best-practices as written, and long-running/compose-y Commands.run(...) belong at the system or RobotContainer level, not inside the subsystem.

Why (concise):
- Subsystems should own hardware, clamping, telemetry and provide command factories (methods that return Command objects). HoodSubsystem does exactly that:
  - It exposes setAngle(Angle), setAngle(Supplier<Angle>), setDutyCycle(...), bumpBy(Angle) — all return Commands (or provide immediate-write helpers).
  - It centralizes clamping and writes via `setPositionImmediate(...)` and the YAMS Arm setter (single source of truth).
  - It publishes a Trigger (`atSetpoint`) and AdvantageKit inputs for telemetry.
- Systems (like `ShooterSystem`) or RobotContainer are the places to:
  - Compose multi-subsystem commands (aim + spin + feed pipelines).
  - Run continuous compute loops (`Commands.run(...)`) that coordinate several subsystems and schedule supplier-backed commands.
  - Bind operator inputs and triggers to command factories.
- Small one-shot runOnce commands that affect only the subsystem (e.g., `bumpBy` implemented with `Commands.runOnce(...)`) are fine to provide from the subsystem as a factory — they are short, local actions the subsystem itself can expose.

Concrete observations about this repo
- HoodSubsystem: good
  - Provides both immediate (imperative) methods and command factories.
  - Bump behaviour now reads the mechanism setpoint and writes via the Arm setter, and clamping is centralized — this is consistent with YAMS-centric examples.
- ShooterSystem: good
  - It contains the long-running compute loop and flywheel-tracking Commands.run(...) and composes aimAndShoot etc. That is the correct place for those run/loop commands.

Recommendations (optional)
- Keep short, single-subsystem helpers (runOnce) as subsystem factories (like `bumpBy`).
- Keep multi-subsystem, long-running, or compute-loop code in the system or RobotContainer (as you already do).
- Add brief Javadoc on each factory indicating intent: “returns a factory; caller schedules” vs “immediate write helper” to avoid future confusion.
- Consider a naming convention to distinguish immediate-write helpers (setPositionImmediate) vs command factories (setAngle / bumpBy) — that lowers cognitive load.

Want me to:
- Add short Javadoc comments to the Hood API clarifying factory vs immediate-write helper? (small edit)
- Scan code and move any remaining Commands.run(...) that live inside subsystems to their owning system/RobotContainer? (I can do an automated pass and propose edits)
- Add a tiny simulation test that verifies multiple bump presses accumulate setpoint as expected?

Pick one and I’ll implement it.

CSGuy57: Can you compare setAngle() and setMechanismPositionSetpoint()

GitHub Copilot: Good question — these two APIs look similar at first but serve different roles in a command-based robot. Below is a concise comparison, the implications for ownership/telemetry/limits, and recommendations for which to use when in your codebase.

Quick summary
- `setAngle(Angle)` — a command-factory: returns a Command that, when scheduled, makes the mechanism track the requested angle using the mechanism's control loop. Use this for scheduled, cancellable, command-based motion (default commands, operator-scheduled movement, autonomous sequences).
- `setMechanismPositionSetpoint(...)` (the YAMS Arm setter) — an imperative write: immediately changes the mechanism's internal commanded setpoint. Use this for immediate writes (e.g., bump buttons that must take effect instantly) or when you intentionally want to mutate the setpoint outside of command scheduling.

Detailed comparison

1) API shape & how they run
- `setAngle(Angle)` (HoodSubsystem):
  - Returns a Command (via `hood.setAngle(clamped)`), so the caller schedules it.
  - The Command typically runs the closed-loop controller while active and yields ownership of the subsystem.
  - Good for composed, long-running behaviors (hold-to-maintain, supplier-backed tracking, sequenced moves).
- `setMechanismPositionSetpoint(...)` (YAMS Arm setter):
  - Immediate, synchronous setter on the Arm object (not a Command).
  - Changes the internal mechanism setpoint right away; no Command lifecycle, no automatic subsystem ownership.
  - Must be used carefully to avoid racing with scheduled Commands that also control the same subsystem.

2) Ownership & command-based conventions
- Commands returned by `setAngle` follow command-based ownership: they require the subsystem while active, preventing conflicting commands from running concurrently.
- Direct setter calls do not require the Scheduler’s ownership semantics; a running command can later overwrite or be overwritten by a setter call (or vice versa). That’s why immediate writes are best for small, intentional one-shot actions (bump-by) where the caller knows what they’re doing.

3) Telemetry & single source of truth
- In this repo you have three related places to read/write setpoints:
  - `hood.setMechanismPositionSetpoint(...)` — Arm setter (mechanism-level write).
  - `smartMotor.setPosition(...)` — write directly to the SmartMotorController (SMC).
  - `smartMotor.getMechanismPositionSetpoint()` — read SMC stored setpoint (we used this because Arm has no public getter).
- Best practice here: keep the mechanism layer (Arm) as the owner of setpoints for writes, but read the SMC when you need a getter. That preserves YAMS semantics (mechanism API owner) while still having a reliable read-source.

4) Limits / clamping
- Commands: your `setAngle(Angle)` clamps the requested value before calling `hood.setAngle(clamped)` — good.
- Direct setter: `setMechanismPositionSetpoint(...)` may or may not do clamping internally (YAMS/Pivot/Arm config has soft/hard limits). It’s safer to clamp before calling the setter so the subsystem enforces soft limits consistently. That’s why we added `setPositionImmediate(...)` which clamps then writes via the Arm setter.

5) Timing / bump accumulation
- If bump logic reads a setpoint from SMC (or mechanism) then writes a bumped setpoint, the read must reflect the last commanded value for per-press accumulation to work.
- Because Arm does not expose a getter, reading the SMC (`smartMotor.getMechanismPositionSetpoint().orElse(getPosition())`) and writing via the Arm setter (or SMC directly) is a practical pattern. We implemented `bumpPositionImmediate` to do exactly that — read the SMC if present, compute new value, clamp, then call the Arm setter via `setPositionImmediate(...)`.

6) Example usage guidelines
- Use `setAngle(Angle)` when:
  - You want a scheduled, cancellable motion that owns the subsystem.
  - You’re composing multiple commands in `ShooterSystem` or `RobotContainer`.
  - You want supplier-backed dynamic tracking (vision, lookup table).
- Use `setPositionImmediate(...)` / `hood.setMechanismPositionSetpoint(...)` when:
  - You need an immediate, atomic change to the commanded setpoint (button bump, manual jog).
  - The action should not create a long-running command or change scheduler ownership.
  - You clamp before writing so the mechanism honors limits.
- Expose a short run-once command factory on the subsystem (like `bumpBy(Angle)` returning `Commands.runOnce(...)`) that internally calls the immediate-write helper. That keeps the factory discoverable while ensuring the write is immediate when the runOnce executes.

7) Edge cases & gotchas
- Race with a long-running Command: if a long-running `setAngle` command is scheduled and you call the immediate setter (bump), the command may overwrite that bump on its next loop. To avoid surprises:
  - Either require the bump command so it interrupts long-running commands; or
  - Ensure the long-running command reads a supplier (so it follows the latest setpoint) and will update to the bumped value; or
  - Structure the system so explicit operator actions cancel or take precedence over automated sequences.
- Arm getter missing: because Arm lacks a public getter, reading the canonical current commanded value requires the SMC read. Doing so is fine but document this design (we added comments).

Conclusion / Recommendation
- HoodSubsystem is using best practices: it exposes command factories for scheduled control and provides immediate-write helpers for instantaneous actions. This matches common command-based patterns and YAMS usage.
- Keep `Commands.run(...)` and other multi-subsystem compute loops in `ShooterSystem` / RobotContainer (as you already do).
- Keep the immediate write helper (`setPositionImmediate`) and use it inside `bumpBy` (via a runOnce) so the factory is discoverable but the write occurs only when the command executes.
- Add short Javadoc to clarify intent for each API: “factory — caller schedules” vs “immediate helper — writes now”.

Would you like me to:
- Add explicit Javadoc to `HoodSubsystem` methods clarifying factory vs immediate helper? (small edit)
- Add a comment/warning where long-running commands could be clobbered by immediate writes (document the race)? (small edit)
- Add a tiny simulation test to validate repeated bump presses accumulate as expected? (bigger edit)

CSGuy57: Do you think writeSetpointImmediate() should be public or private.

GitHub Copilot: Short answer: keep it public — but treat and document it as an explicit imperative helper (not the primary API). Prefer callers to use the command factories.

Why I recommend that
- Your design already provides both styles:
  - moveToAngle(...) / bumpSetpoint(...) are the command-factory (declarative) API that callers should use and schedule.
  - writeSetpointImmediate(...) is an immediate, imperative write that directly mutates the mechanism setpoint.
- Making the immediate writer public gives you useful, low-friction access for:
  - quick tuning tools, one-off calibration routines, test helpers, simulator setup, and CLI/debugging flows that intentionally want an immediate effect.
  - unit tests or simulation code where scheduling a Command would be unnecessarily verbose.
- At the same time, exposing it publicly is safe because:
  - You already centralised clamping inside writeSetpointImmediate(), so all imperative writes still enforce limits and the mechanism remains the single source-of-truth.
  - The command-based/behavioral API remains the preferred path for normal operation; callers who follow best-practices will still use moveToAngle/bumpSetpoint.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: external code might call the immediate writer in places where a scheduled Command would be safer (race/ownership issues).
  - Mitigation: add a clear Javadoc that documents intent and warns callers to prefer factories for scheduled behavior.
  - Optional: add an assertion or telemetry when the immediate write is called during enabled/disabled states if you want extra safety.
- Risk: misuse across packages if you intended the helper to be internal only.
  - Mitigation: make it package-private or private if you truly want to restrict use. That’s safe now because your command factories already provide all needed external paths.

Concrete recommendation
- Keep `public void writeSetpointImmediate(Angle target)` as public.
- Improve its Javadoc to make intent explicit, e.g.:

  - "Imperative, immediate write for tests, tuning, and short-lived operators. Prefer the command factories (`moveToAngle(...)`, `bumpSetpoint(...)`) for normal robot behavior."

- Optionally add a single-line comment or log entry when it runs in normal (enabled) mode to make uses easier to audit during debugging.

If you want a stricter policy instead
- Make it private (or package-private) so callers must use:
  - `moveToAngle(...)` for scheduled moves, and
  - `bumpSetpoint(...)` for per-press adjustments.
- I can make that change now if you prefer to enforce the command-factory-only public surface.

Which would you prefer? I can:
- Keep it public and add the more explicit Javadoc (recommended), or
- Make it private/package-private and update callers (safe since callers already use the command factories).

CSGuy57: Second option

GitHub Copilot: I'll search for any external uses of `writeSetpointImmediate(...)` (to ensure changing its visibility won't break callers), then make it package-private (private) and update its Javadoc accordingly, and run a Gradle build to verify.

Our repo’s vendordep already points to 2026.2.21 and the project builds cleanly, but there are a few behavioral / API areas you should validate before pushing this onto the robot.

What changed (high-level from the release notes)
- Major closed-loop controller update and SMC PID refactor — changed internals of the closed-loop controller and how SMC PID is organized.
- Fixes to velocity control and limit-switch handling on TalonFX/TalonFXS.
- Added LQR and new motion-profile features (including velocity-trapezoidal and separated motion profile config).
- Added live tuning for exponential & trapezoidal profiles.
- Fixed external-encoder offset behavior (now treated as simple offsets).
- Miscellaneous simulation / telemetry fixes and improvements (AdvantageKit examples, telemetry config).
- Several bug fixes (arm starting-position handling, better safety checks).

What I checked in your repo
- vendordep file: yams.json in this repo contains version 2026.2.21 (so the vendordep we ship here already targets the new release).
- Build: after the edits earlier I ran a Gradle build; it succeeded (BUILD SUCCESSFUL).
- Code style: your code uses YAMS APIs (Arm, SmartMotorControllerConfig, SmartMotorController, setAngle, setMechanismPositionSetpoint, simulation simIterate, etc.). I did not find compile errors after switching to the 2026.2.21 vendordep.

Possible impacts and things to validate (ranked)
1) Closed-loop behavior / tuning (HIGH)
   - The closed-loop internals were updated and the SMC PID refactor changes how the controller behaves and possibly how gains apply. Expect numeric differences (rise time, overshoot).
   - Action: run the hood/turret/flywheel tuning flows in sim and on the test bench. Verify step response, at-setpoint detection, feedforward performance, and re-tune PID & feedforward if necessary.

2) Limit switches & encoder offsets (HIGH → MEDIUM)
   - Limit-switch fixes are good but may change startup/homing safety behavior. External encoder offset behavior change means any code that relied on “smart” offset handling must be validated.
   - Action: verify homing routines and safety behaviors on bench hardware and that encoder offsets produce the expected positions.

3) Motion profiles, LQR, and live-tuning (MEDIUM)
   - New profile types (velocity-trapezoidal), separated profile config, and live tuning offer useful features. They shouldn't break code but can be used for improvements.
   - Action: if you plan to use motion profiles, test the new API surfaces and update configuration code if you switch modes.

4) Telemetry & tuning UI (LOW → MEDIUM)
   - Live tuning and telemetry improvements could change default telemetry names or how AdvantageKit integrates.
   - Action: confirm that your AdvantageKit/Logger dashboards still show the same fields and that any live-tuning controls behave correctly.

5) API/breaking changes (LOW)
   - Many PRs are additive or internal refactors. We saw items that might be breaking (SMC PID refactor) but our build passed under the vendordep version in the repo.
   - Action: run a full compile, then a full test/passive simulation run. Keep an eye for deprecation warnings at compile time.

  1. On bench: exercise hood step moves, bump-by per-press, and confirm atSetpoint tolerances.
  2. Test limit switch behavior and homing.
  3. Verify flywheel/turret interactions and end-to-end aimAndShoot pipeline under safe conditions.
  4. If feedforward/PIDs look different, re-tune on bench and record new constants.

Options — what I can do next

CSGuy57: what is velocity trapezoidal? 

GitHub Copilot: Short answer
- A velocity-trapezoidal profile is a motion-profile that plans the commanded velocity over time in three phases: accelerate, cruise at constant max velocity, then decelerate — producing a trapezoid shape when you plot velocity vs time. It’s used to produce predictable, constrained motion (limits on velocity and acceleration) and is ideal when the controller underneath tracks velocity well.

Why teams use it
- Predictable motion that respects max velocity and acceleration limits.
- Simple to compute and implement.
- Useful when you prefer commanding velocity setpoints to a good velocity controller (rather than commanding position setpoints directly).
- When the distance/time is short it naturally becomes a triangular profile (no cruise phase).

Key math (compact)
- Let vmax = maximum velocity, amax = maximum acceleration.
- Time to accelerate to vmax: t_acc = vmax / amax.
- Distance covered during accel: d_acc = 0.5 * amax * t_acc^2 = 0.5 * vmax^2 / amax.
- If total distance D >= 2 * d_acc: trapezoid (accel → cruise → decel).
  - cruise time t_cruise = (D - 2*d_acc) / vmax.
  - total time T = 2*t_acc + t_cruise.
- If D < 2 * d_acc: triangular profile — you never reach vmax; peak velocity vp = sqrt(D * amax).

(KaTeX)
- t_{acc} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}
- d_{acc} = \tfrac{1}{2} a_{max} t_{acc}^{2} = \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 a_{max}}
- If D < 2 d_{acc}, v_{peak} = \sqrt{D \cdot a_{max}}

Practical notes / tradeoffs
- Commands velocity setpoints to the lower-level velocity controller. So you need a robust velocity controller (SMC closed-loop, motor controller velocity loop, or YAMS SMC velocity controller).
- Jerk: basic trapezoidal profiles have step changes in acceleration at phase transitions → jerk discontinuities. If jerk must be limited, use S‑curve profiles (smooth transitions) instead.
- Position vs velocity profiling:
  - Position-trapezoid: plan position over time (position is primary). The profile produces velocity setpoints by differentiating position. Common when you have good position control.
  - Velocity-trapezoid: plan velocity directly and send those velocity setpoints into a velocity controller. Simpler if you already have a strong velocity controller (e.g., flywheels, conveyors).
- Feedforward: velocity profiles often work best with feedforward (kV, kA), especially to meet the velocity/accel shape.

Implications for this project (given YAMS 2026.2.21)
- YAMS added native support for velocity trapezoidal profiles and live tuning. That means:
  - We can command a mechanism with a velocity-trapezoid if the SmartMotorController/Arm supports it.
  - For subsystems already using YAMS (hood, turret, flywheel), velocity-trapezoidal may be a good fit where velocity control is primary (flywheel, spindexer, spinners).
  - For arm-like mechanisms (hood) that also experience gravity and torque, you may prefer position-based profiling plus good feedforward and gravity compensation, or use velocity trapezoid with appropriate feedforward that models gravity/torque.
- Because YAMS also refactored SMC PID and added velocity profiles, you should:
  - Validate velocity-controller behavior in sim + bench before deployment (tuning likely needed).
  - Try the new velocity profile in sim on a non-critical subsystem (spindexer / conveyor) to get comfortable.
  - Use live tuning to adjust profile params (vmax/amax) and feedforward gains.

Examples of when to use it here
- Use velocity-trapezoid for:
  - Flywheel spin-up with controlled accel limits to avoid power spikes.
  - Spindexer / conveyor / any roller where velocity is the primary controlled variable.
- Prefer position-profile (or arm-specific feedforward + gravity compensation) for: hood if you need precise angle tracking under gravity; turret if you need precise positioning but you could use velocity-trapezoid to move the turret quickly if followed by position hold.

Suggested verification steps I can run for you
- Quick sim demo: exercise a velocity trapezoid on a simple mechanism in our sim and show velocity/time and position/time plots.
- Code scan: find candidate subsystems where YAMS velocity-trapezoid could be applied and list them.
- Small prototype: implement a velocity-trapezoid on a non-critical subsystem (spindexer/flywheel) and run it in sim with live tuning hooks.

Which would you like me to do next?
- Run a short sim demo of a velocity-trapezoid (visual/log output).  
- Scan the repo for candidate subsystems and propose where to swap to velocity-trapezoid.  
- Prototype a velocity-trapezoid command for a chosen subsystem (I’ll wire it up in code + run sim).
